hero.title: A learning platform built for university education.
hero.subtitle: Artemis is an open-source learning platform built for university education — instant feedback on programming exercises, smart quizzes, and collaborative tools that keep both students and instructors in sync.

spotlight.title: AI that teaches, not just answers
spotlight.description: Iris, Artemis's built-in AI tutor, guides students through problems with hints and leading questions — drawing on course-specific content to stay relevant. For instructors, it surfaces patterns across student questions and keeps FAQs up to date automatically.

tutor.title: AI that teaches, not just answers
tutor.description: Iris, Artemis's built-in AI tutor, guides students through problems with hints and leading questions — drawing on course-specific content to stay relevant. For instructors, it surfaces patterns across student questions and keeps FAQs up to date automatically.

feedback.title: Instant feedback, every submission
feedback.description: Submit code, a quiz, or a modeling task — and get structured, individual feedback within seconds. Artemis automatically runs test cases, static analysis, and grading criteria so students always know exactly where they stand, without waiting for a tutor.

transcribedLecture.title: Lectures that keep working after class
transcribedLecture.description: Upload lecture videos and Artemis automatically generates searchable transcriptions. Students revisit specific moments, Iris draws on the content to answer questions, and instructors can link units directly to learning objectives.

insights.title: Learning paths that adapt to every student
insights.description: Define competencies, map them to lectures and exercises, and let Artemis build personalized learning paths based on each student's progress — giving struggling students targeted support and advanced learners room to move ahead.

assessment.description: From anonymous, rubric-based manual grading and AI-assisted correction suggestions, to fully automatic evaluation of quizzes and programming submissions.

tutorials.description: Structured small-group sessions with exercise assignments, attendance tracking, and tutor tooling designed to scale across large cohorts.

lectures.description: Upload slides, embed videos, and divide content into structured units, all linked directly to learning objectives.

examMode.description: Online exams with randomized variants, plagiarism detection, and structured grading criteria.

communication.description: Course-wide announcements, discussion threads, and private messaging.

faq.description: Searchable, course-level FAQ library that reduces repetitive queries and feeds directly into Iris for smarter AI responses.

iris.description: An AI-powered tutor that guides students with hints and questions rather than answers.

mobileApps.description: Access courses, exercises, lectures, and discussions on the go.

free.question: Is Artemis free to use?
free.answer: Yes — Artemis is fully open source under the MIT license. You can self-host it or use the hosted version provided by TU Munich for partner institutions.

languages.question: Which programming languages does Artemis support?
languages.answer: Artemis supports Java, Python, C, Haskell, Kotlin, Swift, OCaml, and more. It integrates with popular CI systems to run automated tests for any language.

grading.question: How does automated grading work?
grading.answer: Student code is built and tested in an isolated container on every submission. Test results are shown instantly. Instructors define the test suite; Artemis handles execution and scoring.

scale.question: Can I run exams for 1000+ students?
scale.answer: Yes. Artemis is battle-tested at TU Munich with large cohorts. The exam mode uses question pools to generate unique papers per student and scales horizontally.

iris.question: How does Iris differ from ChatGPT?
iris.answer: Iris is designed specifically for education — it guides students with hints and leading questions instead of giving direct answers. It draws on course-specific content and is integrated directly into the learning workflow.

docs.question: Where can I find the full documentation?
docs.answer: Full documentation is available at docs.artemis.cit.tum.de, including setup guides, feature references, and developer docs.

footer.subtitle: Open-source and continuously improved by a growing community of universities and developers.
footer.description: A learning platform built for university education.
